citation.title: Meaningful human control in drone swarms

extraction_metadata.notes: Bachelor's thesis (Linköping University, Spring 2026). Coded from the Introduction, Related Work, full Method, full Results (4.1-4.4), and full Discussion/Conclusion (5.1-6); Appendices A-G (consent form, HMI intro text, task briefings, inject timelines, CDM probes, per-participant timelines) were not read in full given their supplementary nature, so some fine-grained detail from them (e.g. exact HMI intro wording, full per-participant timelines) is not reflected below.

measured_constructs: Other / Not Listed (qualitative control-limitation/usability analysis via the Critical Decision Method)

study_type.justification: Five participants each performed a full simulated military-surveillance mission with a 50-drone swarm, with their behavior screen-recorded and analyzed via the Critical Decision Method and mapped to alert/incident-detection outcomes (Table 4.1) — a real, measured (if small-N and qualitative) user study, making User Study Primary. The thesis's other major, comparably-weighted contribution is a new consolidated Meaningful Human Control (MHC) framework (merging NATO's dimensions with the Comprehensive Human Oversight Framework) and the newly proposed Hard/Soft MHC distinction, a genuinely new conceptual classification scheme, warranting Framework/Taxonomy Paper as Secondary.

summary.task: Five participants individually operated a semi-autonomous 50-drone swarm through a Unity-based ground-surveillance simulation (surface surveillance of a peninsula, its archipelago, and three fictional security-sensitive sites near Västervik, Sweden) via a GUI, with the goal of detecting and reporting potential threats to the sites as early as possible. Each participant was assigned one of three scripted 1-to-1.5-hour scenarios containing pre-timed 'injected' antagonist/vehicle intrusion events (Scenario 3 additionally included a scripted friendly military exercise). Background 'noise' objects (civilian boats, people, cars on predefined trajectories) were present throughout to simulate realistic surveillance clutter.
summary.control: Operators used four categories of indirect/abstracted swarm control rather than direct piloting, following Crandall et al.'s (2017) taxonomy: parametric control (adjusting search-algorithm parameters such as the PSO 'decay' value, or switching between the PSO and Snake sweep algorithms), associative control (selecting individual drones to view live camera feeds or check status), environmental control (defining/editing geographic 'zones' that bound where drones search), and strategic control (creating 'plays' that deploy a chosen number of drones with a chosen algorithm to a zone, and setting a per-object 'tracking policy' — ignore/low priority/neutral/important/very important — that determined how many drones a detected sensor object would keep tracking it, and for how long). No direct/manual piloting of individual drones was available at all — its absence was one of the most frequently raised participant complaints.
summary.participants: 5 male participants (of an original 6; one dropped out), aged 26-50, all employees of GEISTT AB (the simulation's developer) recruited via convenience sampling, all with a computer-engineering background. Some participants had been involved in developing parts of the simulation environment; one participant had no prior experience with the system at all.
summary.results: Across a six-step protocol (consent, written HMI introduction, self-paced trial run, task briefing, the ~1-to-1.5-hour scenario with concurrent think-aloud, and a post-trial semi-structured Critical Decision Method interview), participants performed five recurring activities — accessing drone camera feeds, controlling drone distribution/zone coverage, judging sensor objects, handling alerts, and tactically distributing drones — via varied individual methods, generally in line with previously-documented human-swarm interaction control modes (algorithm switching, parameter changes, environmental/zone-based influence). Detection outcomes varied sharply across participants: one detected 0 of 3 injected incidents (his drones never registered the relevant sensor objects and he received no alerts), while another received alerts for all incidents in his scenario (Table 4.1). Thematic analysis of the trials and interviews identified 32 distinct control-limitation issues, grouped into six themes, which were then mapped onto a consolidated MHC framework (NATO's dimensions of human control situated within the Comprehensive Human Oversight Framework's technical/socio-technical/governance layers and before/during/after-deployment temporal structure).
summary.issues: The 32 identified control issues cluster into six themes. (1) Camera-feed limitations: no zoom or pan/angle control on the live feed, and no way to tell from the map which drone's feed was being viewed or where it was pointing, making targeted reconnaissance harder. (2) Differentiating sensor objects: identical labels for same-type objects caused mix-ups when accessing feeds or changing tracking policies, leading in several cases to faulty situational assessments (e.g. one participant mistook two different cars for one car that had 'moved', another falsely suspected an innocuous sailboat of loitering). (3) Alerts and situational awareness: alert cards were confusing and unintuitive even by the end of a trial; there was often no clear path of action after raising an alert (one participant abandoned an alert because manual investigation was unavailable); overview was sometimes lost when zoomed into one site; not all participants received alerts for incidents that occurred (a technical gap); and no alert was raised when a person entered a no-entry zone in one case. (4) Tracking behavior: drones sometimes kept tracking a now-stationary vehicle instead of switching to follow a person who had exited it, and 'getaway vehicles' were registered as sensor objects but never actually tracked, causing several participants to lose antagonists during the incident phase. (5) Swarm behavior: unwanted flocking/clustering and incomplete area coverage recurred across all trials; participants could not reliably tell whether their corrective actions (adjusting decay, switching algorithms) had the intended effect, and some explicitly distrusted the displayed coverage statistic or the swarm's behavior generally. (6) Zones/plays functionality gaps: no way to delete or edit a created zone, no manual drone control, and no way to highlight priority sub-areas within a zone; the plays-panel's design also contributed to participants sending drones to the wrong zone, canceling the wrong play, or renaming the wrong zone. Mapped onto the consolidated MHC framework, pre-deployment conditions (operator understanding of the system/task/environment; proper prior constraints) were the most clearly unmet — participants were undertrained and had to learn core mechanics (decay, alerts, zone shapes) during the trial itself — while during-deployment conditions (freedom of choice, ability to affect system behavior, situational awareness, predictability of one's own control inputs) were compromised in specific, traceable instances but proved conceptually harder to cleanly map to individual issues. Post-deployment, no participant felt fully responsible for the system's performance, and there was no way to verify system/drone behavior in hindsight beyond re-watching the same information available live.
summary.recommendations: Address the specific interface/behavior gaps directly (manual drone control, camera zoom/pan, map-to-feed correlation, zone editing/deletion, corrected getaway-vehicle and stationary-vehicle tracking logic, post-hoc verification/logging of drone behavior and detections). More fundamentally, invest in operator scaffolding before deployment: clear standard operating procedures, defined operator roles, structured training on the algorithms/parameters (e.g. what 'decay' does), and briefing on the specific environment being surveilled (site layouts, likely intrusion points, baseline 'normal' activity) — since much of the observed control failure traced to insufficient preparation rather than the interface alone. At the theoretical level, the thesis recommends adopting its proposed Hard/Soft MHC distinction: Hard MHC (binary, checklist-style requirements — e.g., does post-hoc verification exist at all) for regulation and system-design gating, and Soft MHC (interpretive, human-factors-grounded quality assessment — e.g., situational awareness, workload, affordances) for iterative, user-study-based evaluation of how well those functions actually work in practice. It also recommends further specifying MHC per use case (for military ground surveillance specifically: operators need area/environment familiarity, some form of manual override, protection from simultaneous unrelated alert overload, and intuitive incident-management tools) and extending this kind of empirical MHC evaluation to other domains (e.g. medical) and participant populations with domain-expert involvement.
summary.autonomous_capabilities: The 50-drone swarm autonomously launches, patrols/searches its assigned zone, and returns/docks without operator oversight (launch and recovery are fully automatic). Drone motion is computed by one of two selectable algorithms: a Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) variant, in which each drone's steering is driven by its own best-known position and a swarm-wide best-known position under a spatial cost function that penalizes revisiting recently-covered 3-D grid blocks (with a configurable 'decay' rate at which that penalty fades); or a 'Snake' algorithm, a parallel shifted line-sweep search that divides the zone into contiguous blocks arranged in parallel line segments statically delegated across the assigned drones. Object detection/classification (person, vehicle, boat, unknown, with a stated classification-confidence value) and camera activation on detection are automatic, as is the drone-to-object tracking behavior implied by an object's assigned 'tracking policy' (i.e., the system autonomously decides how many drones remain assigned to a tracked object based on the policy level, without further operator action).

task_object_spatiotemporal_dynamics.justification: Numerous background 'noise' sensor objects (civilian boats, people, cars) and the scripted intrusion events appear at scattered locations across the operational area over the course of a scenario.

Both background and intrusion-related sensor objects (boats, vehicles, antagonists) move along trajectories and, once identified as suspicious, must be continuously tracked as they relocate (e.g. an antagonist disappearing into a getaway vehicle).

behavioral_relation_to_task_object.justification: Operators must detect and classify sensor objects of initially unknown type/threat level as the swarm surveils the area, the core surveillance task described throughout.
Once a sensor object is judged suspicious, operators elevate its tracking policy to keep drones following it as it moves (e.g. attempting to keep custody of a fleeing antagonist or getaway vehicle).

The operator's terminal action on a confirmed threat is to report/escalate it to a higher authority (the researcher, standing in for a command structure) rather than to avoid, engage/neutralize, pursue further, or block it directly — none of the six existing codes capture a report-to-higher-authority behavioral relation.

team_size.justification: Each participant individually operates a 50-drone swarm in their session.

team_composition.justification: The 50 drones are identical in capability and role, differentiated only by which zone/play they are currently assigned to.
Each session involves a single operator; no multi-human team is present within a trial.

interaction_model.justification: Zone, play, and tracking-policy actions affect groups of drones (an entire play's allocation, or all drones tracking a given sensor object) simultaneously.
Selecting a specific drone's live camera feed via the map or the plays-panel tracking icons is a one-to-one interaction with a single drone.

interaction_directness.justification: All operator actions (defining a zone, creating a play, setting a tracking policy, selecting a camera feed) are explicitly addressed to a specific zone, play, object, or drone via the GUI.

communication_proximity.justification: The operator interacts entirely through a screen-based GUI in a meeting room; the drones/environment are simulated, so there is no physical co-location with real vehicles.

coordination_architecture.justification: Under the PSO algorithm, each drone's motion depends only on its own and the swarm's locally/globally shared best-known positions via a shared cost function, with no leader drone or central per-drone commander described.

Under the Snake algorithm, the search area is divided into parallel line segments by a single computation and then statically delegated to specific drones, a fixed, centrally-computed assignment rather than emergent local coordination.

computational_characteristics.justification: The PSO algorithm explicitly drives drone steering via each drone's personal-best and swarm-best positions under a spatial cost function, the codebook's canonical PSO example.
The Snake algorithm is a deterministic, geometrically-defined parallel line-sweep (lawnmower-pattern) coverage search over pre-divided 3-D blocks, without a learned model or population-based search.

The Tracking Policy mechanism decides how many drones (0, 1, 2, 4, or 5) are assigned to track a given sensor object based on its priority level, a task/role-assignment decision distinct from how any one drone computes its own motion.

human_involvement_level.justification: The swarm searches, patrols, launches, and recovers drones fully autonomously via the selected search algorithm without requiring continuous operator input; the operator monitors via the map/panels and can intervene as needed.

Judging sensor objects, adjusting tracking policy, handling alerts, and reporting confirmed incidents to higher command all explicitly require manual operator action to occur; the mission goal (detect and report threats) is not achieved without this human intervention.
human_involvement_level.condition: threat judgment, alert handling, and reporting

level_of_human_control_abstraction.justification: All available operator inputs (zone boundaries, algorithm choice, decay value, drone count, tracking policy level) are discrete parameter settings that the autonomous swarm then maintains/executes, with the operator retaining a monitoring/safety role (watching for alerts); no continuous or direct per-drone control existed at all in this system, a gap participants explicitly flagged as a missing control mode (control issue #28).

operational_domain.justification: The managed swarm consists of aerial drones, even though the surveilled area and sensor objects span land and maritime environments.

flags.note: Not a coding gap — noted only to confirm: despite a physically-present lab setup (meeting room, monitor, keyboard) and a real researcher present, no physical drone hardware is used, so Setting correctly defaults to Simulated/Remote rather than Lab per the codebook's hardware-presence requirement.

Confirmed-incident handling in this article ends with the operator reporting/escalating to a higher authority, a behavioral relation not covered by Avoidance, Search, Engagement, Pursuit, Defend/Block, or Monitoring/Maintenance.
flags.proposed_code: Report/Escalate Task — the operator's required behavior toward a confirmed task-relevant object/event is to notify or hand off to a higher authority/decision-maker, rather than to personally engage, avoid, pursue further, or block it.

flags.note: The two search algorithms (PSO, Snake) are explicitly described, allowing confident coding, but no other coordination mechanisms (e.g., during tracking-policy reassignment or multi-drone object tracking) are detailed beyond the base search-algorithm behavior.
